content: Hey, I'm really struggling with this lesson 12 material on pretérito indefinido. Elena gave us this worksheet with like 30 irregular verbs and honestly I feel like I'm just memorizing random forms. Like, why is 'ir' suddenly 'fui' and 'ser' is also 'fui'? There's no pattern I can see. How am I supposed to remember all of these before Tuesday's lesson?

I totally get why this feels overwhelming—irregular verb stems in pretérito can seem chaotic at first, but there actually ARE patterns hiding in there. The reason 'ir' and 'ser' both use 'fui' is because they share the same preterite forms (one of Spanish's quirks!). Here's the thing: instead of trying to memorize 30 individual verbs, group them by their stem changes. Verbs like 'tener' (tuv-), 'estar' (estuv-), 'poner' (pus-), and 'haber' (hub-) all follow that -u- pattern. Then you've got the 'i' group: 'venir' (vin-), 'querer' (quis-), 'hacer' (hic-). Once you see those patterns, you only need to remember maybe 5-6 stems, not 30. Elena probably has you on this chapter because it's essential before moving into longer narratives. Want me to help you organize the verbs by stem pattern?

Oh okay, yeah that actually makes sense. So it's like they group into families based on that vowel in the stem. I think I see 'tuve, estuvo, puse' now that you mention it—they all have that 'u' sound. But what about the endings though? Like after I figure out the stem, do I use the regular -é, -aste, -ó endings, or is that messed up too?

Great news—the endings are actually consistent and way simpler than you might think! Once you have your irregular stem (like 'tuv-' from 'tener'), you use the SAME endings as regular preterite verbs: -e, -iste, -o for yo/tú/él, and -imos, -isteis, -ieron for nosotros/vosotros/ellos. So 'tener' becomes: tuve, tuviste, tuvo, tuvimos, tuvisteis, tuvieron. The only tricky part is that the third person singular and plural don't have an accent mark, unlike regular verbs ('hablar' → hablé, but 'tuve' has no accent). So you're really just learning: (1) identify the stem group, (2) apply the standard preterite endings. That's it! Once you nail down maybe 15-20 of the most common irregular stems, you can conjugate them in any person. Elena probably focuses on these because 'tener,' 'hacer,' 'ir,' 'ser,' 'estar,' and 'venir' come up constantly in real conversation.

Okay this is actually clicking now, thanks. One more thing though—I noticed in Elena's examples that some verbs change the stem AND seem to have different vowels in different forms? Like I swear I saw 'supe' but also 'supo'? Am I just misreading or is there another layer to this?

Nope, you're reading it right! That's just how irregular stems look when they're conjugated with the different person endings. With 'saber' (to know), the stem is 'sup-' and then you add the endings: yo supe (sup + e), tú supiste (sup + iste), él supo (sup + o). So you're seeing 'supe' and 'supo' because they're different conjugations of the same stem with different endings tacked on. It might look confusing when you're scanning a list, but when you conjugate it yourself in context it'll feel more natural. The stem stays 'sup-' across all forms, it's just what comes after that changes. By Tuesday, focus on drilling these stem groups out loud—say 'tuve, tuviste, tuvo, tuvimos...' five times each. Your brain will lock in the patterns way faster through repetition than just staring at a worksheet. Elena's lesson Tuesday will probably involve using these in sentences, so having the base forms solid will really help you focus on meaning rather than struggling with the forms.

Got it, I'm feeling way more confident now. Should I focus on memorizing all the verbs on the worksheet or just the really common ones Elena mentioned? And what if I blank on one during the lesson—like, is there any way to figure it out on the spot or will Elena think I haven't studied?

Honestly, focus on the common ones first: 'tener,' 'hacer,' 'estar,' 'ser,' 'ir,' 'venir,' 'poder,' 'saber,' 'querer,' 'poner.' Those ten show up in like 80% of conversations and they cover different stem patterns, so you'll recognize similar patterns with less common verbs. If Elena asks about a less common one during Tuesday's lesson and you blank, don't panic—it happens to everyone learning Spanish. You can honestly say something like 'Dame un momento, necesito pensar en el stem' (give me a moment, I need to think about the stem). Elena appreciates when students recognize they need to think rather than just guessing randomly. Plus, if you can explain your thinking ('Oh wait, that's in the 'i' stem group, so it should be vin- or quis-?'), she'll see you understand the SYSTEM, which is way more important than having 30 verbs memorized. That said, do try to drill those ten core ones until they're automatic—they're the foundation for everything else in Spanish conversation going forward.
